Documentation for the AMS AS7000 SoC, detailing its boot process, loader, and application firmware.

# Purpose
This document provides detailed information about the AMS AS7000, a Cortex-M0 System on Chip (SoC) with specialized peripherals, which can be programmed to function as a heart-rate monitor. It explains the boot process of the AS7000, which involves a ROM bootloader and a loader application, detailing the conditions under which the chip transitions from one stage to another. The document also describes the loader's functionality, which allows new applications to be programmed into the main flash memory over I2C using Intel HEX records. It specifies the supported HEX record types and the conditions under which the loader exits. Additionally, the document outlines the application firmware's operation, emphasizing its control over the system and the necessity for the firmware to implement the I2C register map as part of the communication protocol. The document clarifies that the application firmware must handle specific tasks, such as recognizing commands to reset into the loader, due to the absence of an operating system.
# Content Summary
The document provides technical details about the AMS AS7000, a Cortex-M0 System on Chip (SoC) with specialized peripherals, which can be programmed to function as a heart-rate monitor. The AS7000 does not come with preloaded firmware, requiring developers to program it with the necessary firmware.

The AS7000 features 32 kB of flash memory for the main application and a reserved flash area containing a loader application. It also includes a ROM first-stage bootloader. The boot process begins when the chip is woken from shutdown by pulling the GPIO8 pin low. The ROM bootloader checks for the presence of a loader application and, if available, transfers control to it. If the application is valid, it remaps the flash to address 0 and jumps to the application. If neither condition is met, the bootloader waits on UART.

The loader application allows new applications to be programmed into the main flash via I2C using Intel HEX records. Supported record types include Data, EOF, Extended Linear Address, and Start Linear Address. The loader requires HEX records to specify addresses in the range 0-0x7FFF and to be sent in strictly increasing order. The maximum record size is configurable, with a default of 256 bytes. The loader exits upon receiving an EOF record, an unrecognized valid HEX record, invalid data, or after a ten-second timeout without receiving records.

The application firmware operates without an operating system, using a simple main loop. It is responsible for implementing the I2C register map as part of the communication protocol. The Application ID register is polled by the main loop to manage module operations. The firmware must recognize specific values, such as 0x01, to reset into the loader. GPIO8 has no special function while the firmware is running.
